The finding (#107, MEDIUM) is that putting a similarity/embedding matrix into BaselineArtifact.contents as nested Python lists triggers freeze()'s element-by-element recursion (an O(n_cells) parallel nested-tuple copy + hash() per float). The suggested fix was to steer builders toward numpy/array.array so freeze()'s O(1) (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor fast path is taken (and/or add a shallow-immutable array container).

The applied fix (commit d0154e1) does exactly the steering option and is sound, not a hack:
- It adds cosine_similarity_matrix_array() in src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py (exported via __init__.py) returning a numpy ndarray with NO nested-list boxing, explicitly documented to be stored in baseline contents because "the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list."
- _frozen.py itself is unchanged vs main — correctly so, because the O(1) array-like descriptor path already exists there (added by #19). The root cause is not a freeze() bug but builders feeding it nested lists; the right remedy is providing/steering to the array form, which is what was done. The engine never internally builds a matrix into contents (verified: no such construction in src/), so there is no remaining internal slow-path default to fix.
- Appropriateness verified empirically against the venv: freeze(np.zeros((2000,2000))) -> a 3-tuple descriptor in 0.009s; freeze of the equivalent nested list -> 1.54s (167x slower). The fix genuinely eliminates the cost for builders that adopt the array primitive.

Complete: yes. Two regression tests were added in tests/test_baseline.py: test_cosine_matrix_array_matches_list_and_is_ndarray (the array variant returns an ndarray and agrees numerically with the list form, incl. empty case) and test_freeze_keeps_ndarray_opaque_o1 (asserts freeze(np.zeros((100,100))) yields a 3-tuple (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor with shape (100,100), NOT a recursively tuple-ized copy). Both pass. The second test would catch a regression: if the array fast path in freeze() were removed, an ndarray (not list/tuple/Mapping) would fall through to the hash() check and raise TypeError (ndarray is unhashable), failing the test loudly; and if the descriptor shape were dropped the len/shape assertions fail.
